skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Use the inference.sh CLI to discover and run cloud AI apps for images, video, LLMs, search, 3D assets, and automation.
skill.summary: Guides agents through inference.sh CLI workflows for cloud AI apps.
skill.author: inference-sh-0

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly found extensive CLI command examples, network use, API key setup, and filesystem writes. I found no prompt injection, hidden code execution, or credential exfiltration intent, but the skill needs a marketplace warning because it guides agents to run an external cloud CLI and includes curl-to-shell installation commands.

medium_findings.title: Network Installer Pipes to Shell
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends downloading an installer from the network and piping it directly to sh. This is risky because users cannot inspect or pin the script before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit in multiple install examples. The risk is real, although it is documented installation guidance rather than hidden execution.

medium_findings.title: External Cloud CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally allows Bash access for infsh commands and provides many examples that run remote AI apps. This can send user prompts, files, and task data to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool scope and run examples clearly delegate work to an external CLI and cloud platform. This matches the skill purpose, so it is elevated risk but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: API Key Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The setup guide instructs users to place an inference.sh API key in an environment variable. This is a legitimate pattern but increases impact if logs or shell history expose secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly document API key use. I found no evidence that the skill exfiltrates the key, but secret handling remains a meaningful operational concern.

low_findings.title: User-Directed Filesystem Writes
low_findings.description: Several examples save app inputs, app lists, task results, or shell completions to local files. These writes are explicit user workflows, but they can modify the local environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem access is visible in documentation examples and appears user-directed. The hidden-file alert is mainly shell completion setup, not stealth persistence.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Trigger Network Alerts
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are mostly product documentation, image, and result URL examples. I found no evidence that these links are used for covert data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links or example output locations. There is no code path that silently sends local data to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to match text fragments in markdown, tables, or API key wording. I found no cryptographic implementation or weak hash usage in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts are prose, markdown tables, or environment variable guidance. No evidence found for MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak cryptographic code.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl to sh Installer Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends executing a downloaded installer with sh. Users should inspect installer contents and prefer pinned package managers when available.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present verbatim in install instructions. The security impact is clear even though it is not concealed.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI With Cloud Side Effects
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill routes agent actions through infsh, including AI generation, search, app deployment, and Twitter automation examples. These operations can incur costs or publish content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands and categories show remote side effects beyond local documentation. The risk is constrained by the explicit infsh tool scope.

content.user_title: Run Cloud AI Apps from Your Agent
content.value_statement: Teams need one interface for many AI models and app runtimes. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use the inference.sh CLI.
actual_capabilities: Lists, searches, and filters inference.sh apps by category, name, and featured status.
Runs cloud AI apps through infsh with file inputs or inline parameters.
Tracks long-running tasks and saves task results for later review.
Provides setup guidance for login, API key use, updates, and shell completions.
Covers common app types, including image, video, LLM, search, 3D, audio, and Twitter workflows.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, account access, and valid authentication before commands can run.
Sends prompts, files, and task metadata to inference.sh or connected app providers.
Does not provide local model execution or offline GPU processing.
Some workflows may create costs, publish content, or depend on third-party service availability.

use_cases.title: Prototype AI Media Workflows
use_cases.description: Generate images, videos, audio, and 3D assets from one agent-guided CLI workflow.

use_cases.title: Compare Cloud Model Apps
use_cases.description: Search available apps, inspect schemas, create sample inputs, and run model tests with tracked results.

use_cases.title: Automate Repeatable AI Tasks
use_cases.description: Use authenticated CLI commands for search, LLM calls, task tracking, and content automation.

prompt_templates.title: Find an App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use inference.sh to find image generation apps. Show me the best matches and explain what each app is for.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Sample Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a sample input for a video generation app, explain the editable fields, then run it after I approve.

prompt_templates.title: Track a Long Job
prompt_templates.prompt: Run this inference.sh app without waiting, capture the task ID, and check the result when it is ready.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-App Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow that searches the web, writes a prompt, generates an image, and saves the final result paths.

output_examples.input: Find image generation apps for product mockups.
output_examples.output: A ranked list of matching inference.sh apps, with category, purpose, and suggested next command for each app.

output_examples.input: Run a long video generation task and check it later.
output_examples.output: A task ID, current status, expected result type, and a follow-up command to retrieve the finished output.

output_examples.input: Prepare setup steps for CI usage.
output_examples.output: A concise checklist covering CLI availability, API key configuration, authentication checks, and safe secret handling.

best_practices: Review app inputs before sending prompts, files, or private data to external providers.
Use scoped API keys and avoid pasting secrets into prompts, logs, or shared transcripts.
Ask for confirmation before commands that publish content, spend credits, or deploy apps.
anti_patterns: Do not run installer scripts from the network without inspecting the source first.
Do not send confidential files to cloud apps unless the provider and retention policy are approved.
Do not automate social posting or app deployment without explicit human approval.

faq.question: What does this skill control?
faq.answer: It guides agents to use the infsh CLI for inference.sh app discovery, execution, task tracking, and setup tasks.

faq.question: Does it run models locally?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflows run cloud apps through inference.sh and connected providers.

faq.question: What credentials are needed?
faq.answer: Users need an authenticated inference.sh account or an INFSH_API_KEY environment variable for scripted use.

faq.question: Can it create images and videos?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include image, video, audio, 3D, search, LLM, and automation app examples.

faq.question: Is it safe for private data?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prompts, files, and task data may be processed by cloud services outside the local machine.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill has legitimate use, but it includes external command execution, cloud networking, API key handling, and pipe-to-shell install guidance.
